questions.scenario: Which details belong in a release record for `devpolaris-orders-api`?

options.text: Runtime target, candidate, and previous target.
Config changes, traffic plan, watch signals, and rollback target.

questions.explanation: A useful release record explains version, runtime, config, traffic, evidence, and recovery.

questions.scenario: The next incident review says, `I think we changed something around there.` What would have prevented that uncertainty?

options.text: A release record with exact artifact, runtime, config, traffic, signals, and rollback target.
Deleting old revisions immediately after release.

questions.explanation: The release record turns memory into evidence.

questions.scenario: A release record lists the artifact and runtime but omits the rollback target. Why is that a problem?

options.text: During an incident, the team may not know the known working path to restore.
Artifact and runtime evidence are never useful.
Rollback targets are only needed for virtual machines.
Azure requires rollback targets in every resource tag.

questions.explanation: Rollback target is part of release safety because it names the path users can return to.
